title: The price objection is never about price
dek: When a client says it is too expensive, they are telling you the value story is incomplete. Finish it.
heroAlt: The price objection is never about price

blocks.html: It is over budget. Every agent hears it weekly, and most respond by retreating: showing cheaper units, hinting the price might move, quietly agreeing. All three responses accept a premise that is usually false, because the client toured a unit they knew the price of before they got in the car.

People do not tour apartments they cannot afford. They tour apartments they cannot yet justify. Too expensive is rarely a statement about the bank account. It is a statement about the story: the value picture is incomplete, so the number stands alone and looks large.

blocks.text: Clients do not need a lower price. They need a better answer to what the price buys.

items.title: Total the real alternative.
items.html: The cheaper unit plus a gym membership, plus parking, plus a longer commute priced in hours. Comparisons change when they are honest.

items.title: Anchor to the daily number.
items.html: A $300 monthly gap is ten dollars a day. Ask what the extra bedroom, the light, or the walk to work is worth per day. Different question, different answer.

items.title: Return to their words.
items.html: You said the win was never dreading coming home. Which of these two units clears that bar? Let their own criteria close it.

blocks.html: Next price objection, respond with one question before any solution: expensive compared to what? The answer tells you exactly which story is missing.
Coffee Is For Closers, Course 4, drills every major objection with reframes and practice reps, so the answers are ready before the moment arrives.

blocks.lead: Course 4 carries the full objection playbook.
